Core Algorithm

Matchmaking Algorithm

Multiplayer Dynamic Battle Session Matchmaking · US 9,610,504 B2
12
Battle Levels
5
Vehicle Classes
9
Method Steps
Content 4 cards
1
Battle Level Table (Figure 8)
The battle level table defines permissible ranges of tiers for each vehicle type within each battle level. Five vehicle classes (Light Tank, Medium Tank, Heavy Tank, SPG, Tank Destroyer) each have different accessible battle level ranges.

Example: Tier 4 Light vehicles access battle levels 4-10; Medium vehicles access battle levels 4-8; Heavy vehicles access battle levels 4-5; SPG vehicles access battle levels 6-10; Tank Destroyers access battle levels 5-8; Premium USSR Valentine accesses battle levels 4-5. Higher tier vehicles cannot access lower battle levels, nor can lower tier vehicles access higher battle levels.

Key insight: The battle level table is the central data structure that encodes all matchmaking rules. By modifying this table, game operators can rebalance the entire matchmaking ecosystem without changing the algorithm code.
FIG. 8 — Battle Level Table
2
Matchmaking Method Flowchart (Figure 9)
Step 901: Define battle level table. Step 903: Receive and queue battle session requests. Step 905: Create battle session with determined level. Step 907: Identify eligible vehicles from queue. Step 909: Assign vehicles. Step 911: Balance teams. Step 913: Start session.

The battle level for a session can be determined based on the majority of eligible vehicles in the queue, a sequential process of incrementing or decrementing battle levels, or other criteria. If insufficient vehicles are available, the server may wait for more requests or start with minimum players. Multiple instances of the method can run concurrently to process large queue backlogs.

FIG. 9 — Matchmaking Method Flowchart
3
Battle Level Assignment Strategies
Multiple assignment strategies are described: random placement within permissible range, sequential incrementing through levels, and performance-based adjustment using win/loss ratios to move players between easier and harder battle levels.

Random placement: Vehicle placed randomly into any acceptable battle level.
Sequential placement: First game at lowest allowed level, second game one level higher, cycling through the range.
Win/loss-based adjustment: Win/loss ratio determines placement — lower ratios push toward easier battles, higher ratios toward harder battles. A ratio above 2.0 automatically places the vehicle in the highest possible battle level.

4
Progressive Range Expansion (Variable N)
Parameter N defines how many battles a new vehicle must play before accessing its full battle level range. For B ≤ N: C = L + (B-1)((M-L-1)/N). For B > N: C = M. This creates a graduated experience for new vehicles.

Example for Tier 4 SPG (L=6, M=10, N=8): Battle 1 → max level 6, Battle 4 → max level 8, Battle 8 → max level 10. After N battles, the full range is available. This protects new players from immediately facing the most difficult possible opponents when using a new vehicle.

Based on US 9,610,504 B2 (April 4, 2017) · Wargaming.net Limited
